content.text: Schemas in tool-aware prompting are JSON Schema objects that define exactly what arguments a tool accepts: field names, types, value constraints, and which fields are required. The model reads the schema and uses it to construct valid tool calls. A precise schema reduces argument errors by narrowing the space of valid inputs the model can generate; a loose schema leaves the model to guess formats, ranges, and whether optional fields should be included.

field — a JSON Schema object — to describe a tool's parameters. The model validates its own tool call against this schema before emitting it, which catches type mismatches and missing required fields at generation time.
Model inventing values like "urgent" instead of "high"
Overly verbose titles or keys truncated downstream
Out-of-range page numbers, negative counts, zero-value IDs
Freeform strings where a structured format is required

Even with a well-defined schema, it is good practice to validate the model's tool call input programmatically before dispatching it to the real function. This catches edge cases where the model produces a technically schema-valid but semantically wrong argument.

# pip install jsonschema
import jsonschema


def validate_tool_input(tool_name: str, inputs: dict, tools: list[dict]) -> None:
    """
    Validate inputs against the tool's schema before dispatching.
    Raises jsonschema.ValidationError if inputs are invalid.
    """
    schema = next(
        (t["input_schema"] for t in tools if t["name"] == tool_name), None
    )
    if schema is None:
        raise ValueError(f"Tool '{tool_name}' not found in tool list.")
    jsonschema.validate(instance=inputs, schema=schema)


# Example: catch a missing required field before the real dispatch
try:
    validate_tool_input(
        "create_support_ticket",
        {"title": "Login broken", "priority": "high"},  # missing 'description' and 'category'
        [CREATE_TICKET_TOOL],
    )
except jsonschema.ValidationError as e:
    print(f"Validation failed: {e.message}")
    # In a real system: send an error message back to the model so it can retry

Schema precision is directly proportional to tool call reliability. The two highest-value additions are: (1) enum constraints on any field with a fixed valid set — this eliminates the most common class of wrong-value errors; and (2) per-field description strings that explain the field's semantics, not just its type. The type alone tells the model what format to use; the description tells it what the value means and when to include it.

Without a schema, a model generating tool calls has no contract to enforce. It may invent field names, use free-form strings where an enum is expected, omit mandatory arguments, or produce numbers as strings. Each of these errors either crashes the downstream function or silently produces wrong results. Schemas solve this by making the model's output space concrete: the model reads the schema, understands the expected shape, and generates a JSON object that fits it.

The practical improvement is substantial. In production tool-calling systems, adding enum constraints to categorical fields and a required array for mandatory parameters is the single highest-return investment for reliability. A well-defined schema is also documentation: future developers reading the tool definition understand the API contract immediately, without needing to trace through function signatures.
: enum constraints eliminate the most common class of wrong-value mistakes — the model can only produce one of the listed values.
: The model validates its own output against the schema before returning, catching type mismatches at generation time rather than at dispatch.
: Per-field description strings in the schema serve as inline documentation visible to both the model and human developers.
: Server-side jsonschema validation before dispatch catches any slip through, preventing malformed input from reaching real systems.
: Field descriptions in the schema replace explicit instructions in the system prompt, reducing prompt length and duplication.
: A detailed schema for a complex tool can consume hundreds of tokens per API call. With many tools and long descriptions, this becomes meaningful at scale.
Overly rigid schemas can frustrate users
: If a required field cannot be inferred from the conversation, the model may ask clarifying questions or refuse to call the tool, increasing turn count.
: Strict schemas that reject all extra fields can cause failures if the model version slightly changes its output style.
: Schemas must stay synchronized with actual function signatures; a mismatch between schema and implementation is harder to detect than a Python type error.

attrs.alt: JSON Schema tool call flow: user prompt → LLM reads schema → tool call JSON → dispatch, with schema components (type, enum, required, description) and a three-layer validation pipeline

content.text: Add enum constraints whenever a field has a fixed valid set. Add maxLength to any string field that feeds into a database column or UI component with a hard limit. Add minimum and maximum to integer or number fields where out-of-range values would cause errors. Use format: "date-time" or format: "email" when the downstream system expects a specific format — this gives the model a strong hint about the required structure.
when the tool performs side effects (creates a ticket, sends an email, modifies a database row) — incorrect arguments here cause real-world harm.
for exploratory read-only tools where freeform input is acceptable and wrong values simply return no results.
optional fields with complex nested constraints — this can cause the model to overthink whether to include them.

The following example shows a production-grade tool definition for a calendar booking assistant. It uses enum to constrain meeting types, format hints for timestamps, minimum on integer fields, and a nested attendees array with required sub-fields.
Model invents values like "urgent" instead of "high"
Always add enum when the valid set is fixed
Model guesses semantics; optional fields are omitted or wrong
Every field needs a description explaining when to include it
Model asks many clarifying questions before calling tool
Make only truly mandatory fields required; give defaults for rest
Model writes 500-char summaries into a 80-char title field
Set maxLength matching actual column or UI limit
Model self-validates but occasionally slips; errors reach real systems
Always validate with jsonschema before dispatch
Match the schema to the function signature exactly
: every required parameter in the schema must exist in the function, and vice versa. Use a test that calls the function with the schema's required fields and sample enum values.
Write descriptions that explain when to include optional fields
: not just what they are. "Recipient display name for personalisation — omit if unknown" is more useful than "Recipient name".
: schema descriptions teach the model about the field's semantic meaning, not about how to behave. Move behavioral instructions ("confirm before calling") to the tool's top-level description.
: send prompts that would tempt the model to invent enum values or omit required fields, then confirm the schema catches them.
: store them in source control alongside the functions they describe. When the function signature changes, the schema must be updated in the same commit.

Schema tokens are part of the prompt and count toward the context window and billing. A typical tool schema with a moderate number of fields uses 200–400 tokens. With 10 tools active simultaneously, this is 2000–4000 tokens of overhead per request — meaningful for high-volume systems but rarely a bottleneck at moderate scale.

For high-throughput systems, prefer schemas with concise descriptions (one sentence per field rather than a paragraph) and avoid redundancy between the tool description and per-field descriptions. If you have many tools with similar schemas, factor common sub-schemas into reusable objects defined once and referenced with $ref — though note that the Anthropic API requires schemas to be fully inline (no external $ref resolution).
Structured tool calls with fixed field sets
Low — model can ignore or misinterpret
Complementary: generate schema from Pydantic model
High for REST; not native to LLM tool calling
When wrapping existing REST APIs as tools

Several libraries make schema authoring and validation easier in Python. The jsonschema library provides validation that mirrors the model's own schema checking. Pydantic v2 can generate JSON Schema from Python type annotations, which is useful for keeping schemas synchronized with actual function signatures. The anthropic Python SDK accepts the schema dict directly; no external conversion is needed.

# Generate a tool schema from a Pydantic model
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Literal
import json


class CreateTicketInput(BaseModel):
    title: str = Field(description="Short issue title (max 80 chars).", max_length=80)
    description: str = Field(description="Full problem description.")
    priority: Literal["low", "medium", "high", "critical"] = Field(
        description="Urgency level. Use critical only for complete outages."
    )
    category: Literal["billing", "technical", "account", "feature-request", "other"] = Field(
        description="Category that best fits the issue."
    )


# Generate JSON Schema from Pydantic model
schema = CreateTicketInput.model_json_schema()
print(json.dumps(schema, indent=2))
# Output schema is ready to use as input_schema in tool definition

CREATE_TICKET_TOOL = {
    "name": "create_support_ticket",
    "description": "Creates a support ticket. Use when the issue requires follow-up action.",
    "input_schema": schema,
}


# Validate tool input using the same Pydantic model
def dispatch_ticket(raw_input: dict) -> dict:
    validated = CreateTicketInput.model_validate(raw_input)  # raises on bad input
    # Forward to actual ticketing API
    return {"ticket_id": "TKT-0042", "priority": validated.priority}
between the model and the tool: it defines what fields exist, their types, valid values, and which are required.
: it eliminates invented values on categorical fields, the most frequent class of tool call errors.
description explains semantics, not just type
: tell the model what the value means and when to include it, not just what format it should be.
address the two most common failure modes: missing mandatory arguments and wrong-value categorical arguments.
: model self-validation is strong but not perfect; run jsonschema.validate() before dispatching to any system that performs side effects.
Use Pydantic to keep schemas in sync
: generate the tool schema from the same Python type that validates dispatch inputs, eliminating drift.
Schema tokens cost money at scale
: keep descriptions concise; a schema is read on every API call.
